title: Chapter 9: The Renfield
content:
**AETERNA TECHNOLOGIES — CAREERS PORTAL**
**Application Status**

Applicant: Reyes, Maya
Position: Audio Visual Technician — Palo Alto Campus
Submitted: October 15, 2026
Status: **In Review**
⚠ *Your application has been flagged for expedited review by the Office of the CEO.*
Maya read it three times. *Flagged for expedited review by the Office of the CEO.* The words didn't change.
She turned the laptop toward Chloe.
Chloe's chopsticks stopped mid-air. Her eyes tracked across the screen. The noodles slid back into the takeout container.
"Flagged by the CEO's office." Chloe's voice was flat. Then: "That's not good. That's not good at all."
"You said that twice."

"Because it's twice as not good as regular not good." Chloe pushed away from the counter and started pacing the narrow galley kitchen, her socks whispering against the tile. "Normal applications get reviewed by HR. Maybe a hiring manager if you're lucky. The Office of the CEO doesn't flag AV technician applications, Maya. They flag C-suite candidates. They flag board appointments. They don't flag the person who's going to set up the conference room projectors."
Maya closed the laptop gently, as if the machine could hear them. The kitchen settled into the hum of the refrigerator and the distant bark of a neighbor's dog.
"If they flagged it because of Lumina," Maya said, "they would have rejected it."
"You don't know that."

"I do, actually. Think about it. Aeterna's whole brand is transparency and innovation and we're-making-the-world-better horseshit. If they ran a background check and found something disqualifying, they'd send the polite rejection email. 'We've decided to move forward with other candidates.' That's what corporations do when they want you to go away quietly. They don't flag you for the CEO's attention."
Chloe stopped pacing. "So your theory is—what? They just really need AV techs?"

"My theory is they flagged it because they need AV technicians and I'm qualified. Look at my resume. Ten years of freelance work, references that check out, certifications that are real." Maya paused. "Maybe they don't know who I am yet. Or maybe they do and they think I'm harmless."
"Or maybe they want you inside where they can watch you."
The refrigerator hummed. The dog barked again, further away now.

Maya reached for the laptop and opened it once more. The status page glowed in the dim kitchen. "That's a possibility," she admitted. "But it doesn't change anything. I need to get inside. Flagged, watched, monitored—I still need to be in that building. The only question is whether I walk in through the front door or find another way."
"The front door is how people get eaten."
"Then I'll be the one who walks back out."

Chloe stared at her for a long time. Then she sat down, pulled the laptop closer, and started typing. "Fine. But we're doing this with eyes open. I'm building you a new cover identity so deep it has a credit history."

**INTERNAL — AETERNA TECHNOLOGIES**
**SECURE TERMINAL — EXECUTIVE FLOOR**
**USER: WEBB, M. — CHIEF OF STAFF**
**TIME: 23:07 PST**
The monitor threw pale light across Marcus Webb's face, turning his skin the color of old paper.

He sat in his office on the fourteenth floor of Aeterna's Palo Alto headquarters, a room so precisely organized that the pens on his desk were aligned at a uniform angle. The building was empty. The cleaning staff knew not to service this floor until after midnight—Mr. Webb preferred the silence.

His fingers moved across the keyboard with mechanical precision. Click. Scroll. Click. The applicant file filled the screen: Maya Reyes. Thirty-two. Freelance audio-visual technician. San Francisco address, Oakland phone number. Education: City College of San Francisco, Associate of Arts in Media Technology, 2016. Employment history: scattered contracts, no gaps longer than six weeks, consistent income.
Clean. Almost aggressively clean.

Marcus pulled up her social media footprint. Minimal. An Instagram account with fourteen posts, mostly nature photographs—Point Reyes, Muir Woods, the cliff trails behind UCSF. A LinkedIn profile that matched her resume exactly. No Twitter. No Facebook. No TikTok.

He cross-referenced her known contacts against the Lucy Chen sphere. Nothing. No shared networks, no overlapping geolocations, no friend-of-friend connections in the metadata. Maya Reyes appeared to have zero relationship with the missing woman whose disappearance had briefly trended on Bay Area news feeds.
But something pinged.

The back-end data showed that an IP address associated with Maya Reyes's home network had connected to the TrueCrimeBayArea Discord server on October 12th. A quick search of that server's activity logs—Aeterna had maintained a backdoor into Discord's metadata infrastructure since 2024, a fact not disclosed in any SEC filing—revealed that the IP had interacted with a user called NullPointer.
NullPointer.

Marcus's fingers paused above the keyboard. The fluorescent light above his desk buzzed—a sound most people stopped noticing after a few minutes, but Marcus heard it always, had heard it for centuries, the insect-whine of artificial illumination.
He ran NullPointer's digital fingerprints through the cross-reference engine. The results returned in 1.3 seconds.

Chloe Park. Age twenty-nine. Oakland address. Arrest record: trespassing and unauthorized access to a telecommunications network, 2021—charges dropped. Academic record: MIT, expelled 2019 for unauthorized access to university research servers. Known associate of hacktivist networks in the Bay Area. No known employment. No fixed income.
Marcus's fingers resumed. He opened a secure memo template and began typing.

*MEMORANDUM — CLASSIFIED — EYES ONLY*
*TO: Vlad, A.*
*FROM: Webb, M.*
*RE: AV Technician Applicant — Security Concern*
*Applicant Maya Reyes linked via IP analysis to known hacktivist Chloe Park (alias NullPointer). Park has history of unauthorized access and is currently surveilling Aeterna-related channels. Potential security concern — applicant linked to known hacktivist. Recommend denial.*
He read it back. Correct. Proper. The kind of recommendation he had made a thousand times before—identify the threat, isolate it, eliminate it before it entered the perimeter.
His left hand began to tremble.
Marcus looked at it. A small thing, the trembling. A fine vibration in his fingers, as if something beneath the skin was trying to break through. He pressed his right hand over his left to still it.
The cursor blinked. *Recommend denial.*
He highlighted the word. Deleted it.
*Recommend approval with enhanced monitoring. Opportunity to observe opposition tactics.*

Marcus stared at the sentence. His face, reflected faintly in the dark margins of the monitor, showed nothing. But his eyes were wet. A thin film of moisture along the lower lids, building, not quite spilling. He didn't blink. Didn't wipe.
He was crying and he didn't know why.

There was something—some filament of recognition, maybe, or resistance, or the ghost of a choice—that had moved through him when he typed the word *approval*. As if the hand that wrote *denial* was not his, and the hand that replaced it was someone else's. Someone he used to be, perhaps. Someone from before.
The filament dissolved. The trembling stopped. Marcus sent the memo.
He closed the applicant file and sat in the buzzing dark.

The fluorescent tubes in Marcus Webb's office were the old kind—magnetic ballast, sixty hertz, the frequency that burrows into the inner ear and nests there. The cleaning staff had complained once, years ago, before they learned not to service this floor until after midnight. The smell of the office was the smell of everything that touched it: recycled air, the astringent bite of glass cleaner, the faint chemical note of off-gassing carpet adhesive. Clean. Sterile. A room that had never been warm.
Marcus opened the bottom drawer of his desk.

Inside, beside a stack of branded notepads Aeterna handed out at conferences, sat a small plastic container. The kind an office manager would fill with paper clips or thumbtacks. Translucent lid. Marcus removed it.

Dried insects. Crickets with their legs folded flat against their desiccated bodies. Beetles, their carapaces faded to the color of old tea. A few others—smaller things, harder to identify, reduced by time to generic chitinous shapes.
He picked up a cricket. It was light, almost weightless, crumbling slightly between his thumb and forefinger. A faint gritty texture. He placed it on his tongue.

The taste was dust and salt and something older—something subterranean, the flavor of things that had lived in dark places. His jaw moved. The cricket cracked between his molars with a sound like a seed hull splitting, and the dusty remnants coated the inside of his mouth, mixing with saliva into a paste that tasted of nothing at all. He chewed mechanically. Swallowed.
His face was completely expressionless.

He reached into the container and took out a beetle. This one was larger. The shell was harder. He bit down and felt it give with a crunch that resonated through his teeth, and he chewed, and swallowed, and reached for another.

The fluorescent light buzzed. The office smelled of nothing. Outside the window, the Palo Alto campus was dark—the parking lots empty, the walkway lights casting orange circles on the concrete, the glass front of Building A reflecting starless sky.

Eating insects at midnight in a corporate office. This was what five hundred years of servitude looked like when no one was watching. Not the gothic drama of shadowed castles and howling wolves. This. A man in a dry-cleaned suit sitting under artificial light, methodically consuming arthropods from an office-supply container, his face as empty as a discontinued product page.
Marcus closed the container. Placed it back in the drawer. Closed the drawer.
He turned off the monitor and sat in the buzzing dark a while longer.

"Okay." Chloe set three items on Maya's kitchen table: a portfolio binder, a single sheet of paper, and a phone. "The portfolio contains photographs of AV setups you supposedly designed and installed. Corporate events, hotel ballrooms, a tech summit in San Jose that I found images from and photoshopped your logo onto. The resolution is good enough for print, good enough for a hiring manager, not good enough for forensic analysis—if they reverse-image-search, they'll find the originals, but nobody reverse-image-searches AV portfolios."

Maya picked up the binder and flipped through it. The work looked professional. The setups were real, the competence was real, but the person who supposedly created them was a fiction. The name on the logo—*Maya Reyes, Audio Visual Solutions*—was her real name. The work was real. Only the reason was a lie.

"The sheet is your cover story, condensed," Chloe continued. "Freelance AV tech looking for stability. Heard great things about Aeterna's culture—don't specify what, let them project. Doesn't know anyone at the company. Looking for the next step in her career. Simple. Boring. Not a person of interest."
Maya read the sheet. The words were straightforward, almost bland. She appreciated that. The best lies were the ones that didn't need decoration.
"And the phone?"

"Burner. Looks like a real one—has your contacts, your calendar, some texts with fake friends about fake brunch plans. If they take it during the interview and scroll through it, they'll find what looks like a normal person's normal phone. No Signal. No encrypted folders. Nothing that says 'I'm here to find the blood farm in your basement.'"
"Sublevel B," Maya corrected absently.
"Sublevel B," Chloe agreed. She chewed her thumbnail—a habit she'd never been able to break. "Practice the cover story."

Maya set down the sheet and straightened in her chair. "I'm a freelance audio-visual technician. I've been doing independent contract work for the past ten years. I'm looking for something stable with a company that values technical excellence and innovation. Aeterna's reputation speaks for itself."
"Good. Less stiff."
"I heard great things about the culture here. I don't know anyone at the company personally, but I've followed Aeterna's growth and I'd love to be part of the team."

"Better. Don't oversell. Let your actual competence speak." Chloe leaned forward. "You ARE good at audio. The frequency work you did to decode Lucy's signal—that's real skill. Just... not the reason you're good at it."

Maya nodded. She'd learned to read frequencies because of what happened in the desert, because of the thing that had been done to her hearing. Now that skill was her ticket inside the place that might be doing the same thing to others.
Her phone buzzed.
She looked at the screen. An email notification, sender: careers@aeterna.com.
**Dear Ms. Reyes,**

**We are pleased to invite you for an in-person interview at our Palo Alto campus. Your interviewer will be Marcus Webb, Chief of Staff to the CEO. Please confirm your availability for October 28th at 10:00 AM.**
**We look forward to meeting you.**
**Aeterna Technologies Talent Acquisition**
Maya stared at the name. Marcus Webb. It meant nothing to her—just a name, corporate-sounding, the kind of name you'd find on an org chart in small print.
"Who's Marcus Webb?" she asked.
Chloe was already typing. Her fingers stopped. Then moved faster. She turned her laptop toward Maya.

A photograph filled the screen. A thin man standing half a step behind Alexander Vlad at some kind of press event. The man's suit was immaculate. His posture was perfect—spine straight, shoulders aligned, chin level. But his eyes were hollow, the skin beneath them shadowed not with exhaustion but with something deeper, something excavated. He looked like a man who had been emptied out and then carefully reassembled.

"That's Marcus Webb," Chloe said. Her voice had dropped. "Chief of Staff. He's been with Vlad for—hell, for as long as anyone can remember. Every press photo, every earnings call, every public appearance—Webb is there. A step behind. Never speaking. Never looking at the camera."

Maya studied the face. There was something wrong with it, and it took her a moment to identify what: the man's expression was perfectly neutral. Not pleasant, not stern, not attentive. Just... null. A face optimized for absence.
"He's Vlad's handler," Chloe said. "His fixer. The one who makes problems disappear and arrangements materialize. If Marcus Webb is interviewing you for an AV technician position—"
"Then the CEO's office isn't just flagged my application," Maya finished. "They're running it."
"Vlad already knows you're coming."
The kitchen was quiet. The refrigerator hummed. Somewhere in the building, a door closed.

Maya looked at the email again. October 28th. Twelve days away. Twelve days before she walked into a building that had a blood farm in its basement and a five-hundred-year-old vampire in its penthouse, and sat across from the vampire's most loyal servant to discuss her qualifications for running cable through ceiling tiles.
She picked up the phone. Opened the email. Typed: *I confirm my availability for October 28th at 10:00 AM. I look forward to the interview. — Maya Reyes.*
Sent.
Chloe watched her. "You know what you're doing."
"I know what I'm doing."

"Because if Webb is the one interviewing you, this isn't a standard hiring process. This is an interrogation dressed up in business casual. He'll be reading everything—your body language, your microexpressions, the way you hold your hands. Vlad doesn't send his chief of staff to interview junior technicians unless he wants information."
Maya set the phone down. "Good."
"Good?"
"Then I won't have to pretend I'm not a threat for long."

She said it quietly, without bravado. A statement of fact. Chloe looked at her for a long time, and in the light of the laptop screen, Maya could see that her friend's eyes were wet—different from the way Marcus Webb's had been wet an hour ago and a continent of darkness away. Chloe's tears were the kind you understood. The kind that came from fear for someone you loved.
"Twenty-five days until the IPO," Chloe said.
"I know."
"Twenty-five days until they harvest those women."
"I know."
"Twelve days until you walk into that building and sit across from a man who eats bugs at midnight and serves a monster—"
"I know."
Maya picked up the portfolio binder. The cover was plain: her name, her fake logo, her real competence bound between cardboard and lamination.
"Twelve days," she said. "That's enough time to get ready."

Outside, the Bay Area night pressed against the kitchen window—streetlights and fog and the faint amber glow of a city that had built a trillion-dollar economy on the principle that the future was worth any cost.

In an office fourteen floors above the Palo Alto campus, a man with wet eyes sat in the dark and listened to the fluorescent lights buzz, and did not sleep, and did not dream, and did not remember the last time he had been something other than what he was.
Somewhere deeper in that building, below ground level, Sublevel B waited.
Twenty-five days.
